nativeRange.habitat: Reef-associated, found in lagoons and outer reef slopes at depths of roughly 1-18 m, typically sheltering inside a host anemone.
nativeRange.biotope: Tropical Indo-Pacific reef and lagoon; coral and rocky reef areas hosting bubble-tip and leathery sea anemones.

decorPreferences: live rock with caves and overhangs
open mid-water swimming space to establish a territory
host anemone optional (Entacmaea quadricolor bubble-tip preferred, Heteractis crispa also accepted)

diet.notes: Generally accepts most prepared marine fare. A varied diet of frozen mysis, brine shrimp, marine pellets, flake foods and herbivore preparations is commonly recommended. In the wild has been documented feeding on planktonic copepods and algae.

goodWith: peaceful and semi-aggressive reef fish of similar or smaller size
reef-safe invertebrates including shrimps and snails
avoidWith: other clownfish species in the same tank
small timid species that can be bullied at feeding time
large predatory fish that may eat small clownfish

summary: A bold reef-safe Indo-Pacific clownfish in the Tomato Complex, with a deep cinnamon-red body, blackish flanks and a single bright white or bluish head bar. Native from Indonesia and the Philippines east to Micronesia and the Coral Sea; commonly hosted by bubble-tip anemones.
careNotes: Amphiprion melanopus (Bleeker, 1852) is a member of the Tomato Complex and shares that group's bold, territorial disposition: tends to be peaceful toward unrelated tankmates but can be aggressively territorial toward other clownfish or similarly shaped damselfish. Best kept singly or as a single bonded pair; do not mix with other clownfish species. Like all clownfish it is a protandrous (male-first) sequential hermaphrodite — in a bonded pair the larger fish is the female and the smaller is the functional male. Host anemones are not required in captivity, but the species readily associates with Entacmaea quadricolor (bubble-tip) and Heteractis crispa, and rarely with Heteractis magnifica, when offered. Juveniles display a thin white head bar that often broadens and shifts toward bluish in adults; flanks and pelvic fins darken to black while the head and abdomen retain a rich cinnamon-red colouration. Adult-size reports vary substantially across sources (LiveAquaria ~10 cm / 4 in, ORA ~8.9 cm / 3.5 in for females, FishBase 12 cm TL, generalist guides 13-15 cm / 5-6 in) — the 9-12 cm range here is the cross-source average, biased toward the larger reported figures. Tank-size guidance also varies (LiveAquaria 30 US gal / ~113 L for a single fish); 150 L was chosen as a cautious round-up that comfortably houses a pair of this stockier, more territorial Tomato Complex clownfish with a host anemone and reef rockwork. Generally considered very hardy and a good entry-level marine fish; captive-bred specimens (widely available from ORA, Proaquatix and Sea & Reef) tend to be more acclimated to prepared foods and aquarium conditions than wild-caught individuals. New aquarists are commonly advised to add the cinnamon clownfish last so that more timid tankmates can establish before it claims a territory.
breedingNotes: Has been reared in captivity at commercial scale (ORA, Proaquatix, Sea & Reef). Generally considered moderate difficulty for an experienced aquarist, comparable to other tomato-complex clownfish. Egg-layer that deposits demersal eggs on a cleaned flat surface (typically near the host anemone); the male guards and aerates the clutch. Clutches average around 250 eggs per spawn. Eggs hatch in roughly 8-11 days depending on temperature; larvae require rotifers followed by enriched newly-hatched brine shrimp in a dedicated rearing tank.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary species page (Proaquatix Captive-Bred Cinnamon Clownfish). The listed root URL https://www.liveaquaria.com/collections/clownfish did not surface this species on its first catalog page; the canonical product page was located via site search.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked scientific name (Bleeker, 1852), family (Pomacentridae), max size (12 cm TL), preferred temperature range (25.1-29.3°C, mean 28.7°C), Indo-Pacific distribution (Indonesia, Philippines, PNG, Coral Sea, Solomon Islands, Vanuatu, New Caledonia, Palau, Micronesia), depth range (1-18 m), wild diet (planktonic copepods and algae), and host-anemone associations (Entacmaea quadricolor, Heteractis crispa, occasionally H. magnifica). FishBase lists max age of 5 years; captive lifespan is typically longer and the species variant uses the longer captive figure.

additional.name: ORA (Oceans, Reefs & Aquariums)
additional.notes: Third-source confirmation of Tomato Complex placement, color phases (black saddle on red body, yellow/orange fins, blue head stripe in adults vs. white juvenile stripe), New Caledonia origin (also marketed as Coral Sea Clownfish), and breeding biology (clutches averaging ~250 eggs with larval development comparable to Tomato Clownfish).

additional.name: AquariumDomain species profile
additional.notes: Fourth-source confirmation that A. melanopus is one of the larger Western Pacific clownfish (5-6 in / 13-15 cm reported), semi-aggressive temperament (notably more territorial than A. ocellaris), recommendation to add last so timid tankmates establish first, and that host anemones (E. quadricolor, H. crispa) are preferred but not required for captive-bred specimens. Used in the adult-size cross-source spread.

additional.name: Reef2Reef wiki
additional.notes: Designated secondary in source-urls.json for fish-saltwater slice. Per-species wiki page not separately verified beyond the general clownfish discussion thread; AquariumDomain and ORA were substituted as additional cross-verification sources.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive-bred at commercial scale by ORA, Proaquatix and Sea & Reef. Demersal egg-layer; clutches of around 250 eggs are common. Larvae need rotifer cultures followed by enriched brine shrimp in a dedicated rearing tank.
